summary: Gruber weighs in on Apple's decision to replace the pistol emoji with a water pistol in iOS 10. He acknowledges the cross-platform compatibility concerns raised by Emojipedia's Jeremy Burge and Ben Mayo, who argue Apple should have hidden the emoji rather than changed its meaning. Gruber ultimately supports Apple's choice, arguing that changing the glyph to a toy is a deliberate statement against gun culture, and predicts other vendors will follow suit. He also shares insider details about Apple internally debating the direction the water pistol faces to pair well with other emoji.
key_points: Critics argue changing the pistol to a water gun creates dangerous cross-platform miscommunication, since the same code point would show a real gun on other platforms
The alternative proposal was to hide the pistol emoji from Apple's keyboard entirely and lobby Unicode for a separate water pistol code point
Gruber supports Apple's approach as a deliberate cultural statement and predicts other vendors will follow within a few years
Microsoft ironically switched their pistol emoji from a toy ray gun to a realistic revolver the same week Apple went the opposite direction
Apple internally debated flipping the water pistol's direction to pair with the splash emoji, ultimately deciding to flip the splash instead
money_quotes: Hiding the pistol from the emoji keyboard is timid. Changing it to a toy is a statement, indicating that Apple wants no part of the gun culture here in the U.S.
Microsoft just can't catch a break lately — even when they're ahead of the curve they find a way to fall behind.
If they do otherwise, they should drop the glyph completely, and display the 'missing glyph' box for the pistol's code point.
If Apple does change the splash direction, it will also pair better with the much-beloved eggplant.
I think it'll all work out, and other vendors will follow Apple's lead and this glyph will be represented by a toy gun everywhere within a few years.
key_insight: Apple replacing the pistol emoji with a water gun is a bold cultural statement rather than a compatibility misstep, and Gruber predicts the industry will follow Apple's lead.
